items.q: What goes between 'veo' and 'mi amigo' in 'I see my friend'?
items.why: Students omit the personal a. When the direct object is a SPECIFIC PERSON (or pet), Spanish inserts 'a' before it. Omitting it sounds odd. 'Veo a Juan' (not 'Veo Juan').

items.q: Where do object pronouns go in 'I see him' (lo veo)?
items.why: Students apply English word order. Spanish places object pronouns BEFORE conjugated verbs. They can attach to infinitives, gerunds, and positive commands - but not after a conjugated verb in normal sentences.

items.q: Which Spanish 'you' do you use with a stranger or elderly person?
items.why: Students use tú with everyone like English 'you'. Spanish distinguishes FORMAL (usted) for politeness/distance and INFORMAL (tú) for friends/family. Usted takes third-person verb forms, which trips students up.
It's diversas. “diverso” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which country declared itself plurinational in 2009?
items.why: Correct answer: Bolivia. “Ecuador” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many million speak Quechua (nearest 1)?
items.why: Exact answer: 8 million. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
